transcript: Yeah, I had to get a coworker to change how they were doing something even though I wasn't their manager. I just talked to them about it, explained why it mattered, and they came around. It worked out and the project finished on time.
strengths: Direct and confident in tone — no hedging
Gets to the point without rambling
gaps: Zero specificity — no role, project, person, or outcome named
How the influence worked is completely absent
one_specific_improvement: Name the context in your next attempt: your role, the type of project, and what the other person was doing — one sentence each for Situation and Task before you describe what you did.

transcript: At my last job I was on a cross-functional product launch team. One of the engineers kept missing internal deadlines by a few days, and it was creating a ripple effect on the rest of us. I had no authority over them, so I set up a one-on-one to talk through it. I showed them how the delays were affecting the overall timeline and asked what was getting in the way. Turned out they were being pulled into support work by another team. We agreed on some changes — protected work hours and earlier communication when something was going to slip. After that the project got back on track and we launched on time.
strengths: Confident and clear delivery — no hedging language
Good instinct: showed data and asked a diagnostic question before proposing a fix
gaps: STAR structure implicit but not explicit — Situation and Task blend together
Specificity still low: timeline, team composition, and agreements are all unnamed
Action is described in one sentence — the conversation itself needs more detail
one_specific_improvement: Add one specific detail to your Action: name the exact question you asked or what the timeline visual showed — make the reader picture the moment you turned the conversation around.

transcript: Situation: So this was on a product launch I was coordinating — maybe a year and a half ago, I think. We had a cross-functional team and one of the senior engineers was delivering things a few days late pretty consistently. I wasn't their manager or anything, so I had to figure out another way to handle it. Task: I guess my goal was to get the project back on schedule without making things awkward. I wasn't totally sure I was the right person to bring it up, honestly, but someone had to. Action: I put together a timeline — or at least a rough version of one — showing how the delays were stacking up. I'm not sure how clear it was, but I tried. I asked the engineer if we could chat informally. When I mentioned the delays, I found out they were being pulled into another team's support queue without much warning. That was something I hadn't anticipated. We talked through some options. I suggested blocking mornings for project work and flagging issues earlier, and they seemed okay with that, I think. I also moved a couple of lower-priority tasks off their plate. Result: Things improved after that, and we did ship on time. I think it helped — though I'll admit I was second-guessing myself a lot during the conversation. I'm not always sure I handled the framing perfectly, but the outcome was good.
strengths: More specific detail: mentions blocking mornings, support queue, task redistribution
gaps: Confidence language has notably deteriorated — I guess, I think, I'm not sure, I wasn't totally sure appear repeatedly
Task framing undermines credibility: I wasn't totally sure I was the right person signals doubt to the interviewer
Result hedged: I think it helped rather than stating the outcome directly
one_specific_improvement: Rewrite your Task sentence without any qualifying language — state your goal as a decision, not a doubt. Replace I guess my goal was with My goal was, and cut I wasn't totally sure I was the right person entirely.

transcript: Situation: About eighteen months ago I was coordinating a product launch with a six-week runway. The team had two engineers, a UX designer, a QA lead, and a marketing manager — none of them reported to me. One of the engineers, a senior on their core team, was delivering modules two to three days late on average. I tracked it across four consecutive sprints, so it wasn't a one-off. Task: I wanted to address it without escalating to management. I've seen that go badly before and I didn't want to damage the relationship — though honestly, looking back, I'm not sure my instinct to avoid escalation was always the right one. Maybe I should have looped someone in sooner. I'm not entirely sure. Action: I built out a dependency map — each delayed deliverable and the downstream tasks it was blocking, with specific dates. I know that sounds thorough, but I wasn't confident it would land the right way. I was worried it might come across as accusatory even though I was trying to make it neutral. I asked for an informal coffee. I led with questions — asked how things were going on their end. I said something like, I wanted to share something I've been noticing, and I hope it's okay that I bring it up directly. Looking back, that framing might have been a bit hesitant. What I learned was that they were being assigned to critical support incidents tagged P1 by a different team lead, with no advance notice and no capacity buffer. We landed on a few agreements: they'd block 9am to 12pm daily, send me a Slack message by EOD if a deliverable was at risk, and I'd absorb two lower-priority items. Result: Delivery consistency improved over the following four weeks and we launched on time. I think the conversation helped, but I honestly still wonder if I could have handled it better. The outcome was good, but I'm not sure the path I took was the most efficient one.
strengths: Highest specificity so far: four sprints, P1 incidents, 9am-12pm blocks, EOD Slack, two redistributed items
Good diagnostic instinct: dependency map with specific dates, listening before presenting
gaps: Confidence language is at its lowest point — I'm not sure, maybe I should have, I wasn't confident, I honestly still wonder all appear in the same answer
Self-doubt injected into Task and Result actively undercuts an otherwise strong answer
Retrospective second-guessing in the Result shifts the close from an outcome to an apology
one_specific_improvement: Remove every sentence in Task and Result that questions your own judgment — the interviewer wants to hear what you did and what happened, not your post-hoc doubt. Save self-reflection for the What I learned section and keep it forward-looking.

transcript: Situation: About eighteen months ago I was the project coordinator for a product launch with VP-level visibility and a six-week runway. The cross-functional team included two engineers, a UX designer, a QA lead, and a marketing manager — none of them reported to me. One of the engineers had delivered modules two to three days late across four consecutive sprints. Those delays were consuming our testing buffer and pushing back the design review and marketing build-out. Task: I needed to change the pattern without formal authority and without damaging the collaboration. My instinct was to avoid escalating to their manager — I've seen that approach create defensiveness. But I'll be honest: partway through this situation, I started questioning that instinct. I still think the path I chose was right, but I held onto some uncertainty about that even after the project wrapped. Action: I built a dependency map — each late deliverable, the downstream tasks it blocked, and the days of buffer burned. I asked the engineer for a coffee, invite said only wanted to sync on the project. I opened by asking how things were going. They told me they were being assigned to P1 support incidents by a different team lead with no advance notice. That changed my framing. I shared the dependency map as shared context, then asked: What would make it easier to protect time for this project? They drove the solution: blocking 9am to 12pm, flagging slips by EOD, asking their team lead for 24 hours advance notice on P1s. I absorbed two lower-priority deliverables and set up a weekly 15-minute check-in. Result: Delivery improved within the first week. When one external incident arose, they flagged it two days early and we adjusted without downstream impact. We launched on time. The engineer said afterward the conversation made them feel respected rather than managed. I'm less confident about my timing — I still wonder if I acted a sprint too late — but the approach itself I'd repeat.
strengths: Full STAR with maximum specificity throughout — VP visibility, four sprints, P1 incidents, named time blocks, weekly check-in
Action is the strongest yet: dependency map, listening-first, solution co-created
gaps: Confidence partially recovered but still not strong — I'll be honest and I still wonder if I acted a sprint too late appear in Task and Result
The self-doubt sentence in Task breaks the momentum right before the Action
one_specific_improvement: Cut the I'll be honest paragraph from Task entirely — it adds no information the interviewer needs and costs you credibility. Move any genuine reflection to a single What I learned line at the very end, framed as growth rather than doubt.
